content.assessment: From a demographic lens, this proposal leaves the spatial and cohort-structural drivers of track viability untouched. It operates entirely on admissions mechanics within an unchanged 6/8-year structure, but the evidence shows selective tracks are already an urban/regional-center phenomenon with minimal rural uptake (40% of primary schools, 60% in villages, have zero exam attempters). SES-weighted admissions reform cannot address the underlying fact that rural cohorts are contracting toward numerical non-viability for any track configuration, selective or not. The reform is a compositional fix layered on a base that is itself demographically eroding, and does nothing to alter the trajectory described by the accelerating national fertility decline.
opportunities: Could be paired with explicit rural-outreach targeting informed by the demographic contraction data, since disadvantaged-school non-participation is concentrated in exactly the regions facing steepest cohort decline
strengths: Does not require new school-network capacity decisions, so it is not disrupted by cohort-driven capacity mismatches at the margin
Compatible with any future planning horizon since it does not depend on assumptions about cohort size
threats: Structural 20+ year cohort decline will continue to shrink the rural applicant pool regardless of admissions design, progressively hollowing out any equity gains achieved through exam redesign in exactly the areas most needing them
weaknesses: Ignores the spatial concentration finding that selective tracks are functionally an urban phenomenon, so admissions reform benefits chiefly already-better-resourced regional-center schools
No mechanism addresses rural settlements with only 50-70 school-age children where even a single viable intake class is becoming numerically untenable
